answers.intake_q1: When did the elbow pain start?
answers.intake_q2: Can you describe the location of the pain on your elbow and if it radiates anywhere?
answers.intake_a2: outside and it doesn't radiate anywhwere
answers.intake_q3: Any recent injury or trauma to the elbow?
answers.intake_a3: no, but the pain started when I was playing tennis
answers.intake_q4: Any swelling, redness, or warmth around the elbow?
answers.intake_q5: Any numbness, tingling, or weakness in your arm or hand?
answers.intake_q6: Any difficulty moving your elbow or pain with specific movements like gripping or lifting?
answers.intake_q7: Any fever, chills, or other systemic symptoms since the pain started?
answers.intake_q8: Have you tried any treatments or remedies for the elbow pain, such as rest, ice, or medications?
answers.intake_a8: icing the first day and rest since then
answers.intake_q9: Any prior history of elbow problems or repetitive strain injuries?
answers.intake_summary: Patient has had lateral elbow pain for 3 weeks, localized to the outside of the elbow, started with tennis activity, no trauma, no swelling or systemic symptoms, pain worsens with lifting, no prior elbow issues. Initial treatment with rest and ice has been tried.

note.soap:
S: The patient is a 45-year-old female presenting with a 3-week history of pain localized to the outside of her elbow. The pain began while playing tennis and has no associated trauma. She reports no swelling, redness, warmth, numbness, tingling, or weakness. The pain worsens with lifting but does not radiate. She has tried icing on the first day and has rested since then without improvement. There are no systemic symptoms such as fever or chills, and no prior history of elbow problems or repetitive strain injuries.

O: Phone evaluation only; no physical exam or vitals available.

A: The clinical presentation is consistent with lateral epicondylitis (tennis elbow), characterized by lateral elbow pain exacerbated by lifting and activity involving wrist extension and gripping. The absence of trauma, swelling, or systemic symptoms supports this diagnosis.

Differential:
1. Lateral epicondylitis (tennis elbow)
2. Radial tunnel syndrome
3. Osteoarthritis of the elbow
4. Referred pain from cervical radiculopathy. Patient is not pregnant.

P: 
 - First-line medication: Nonsteroidal anti-inflammatory drugs (NSAIDs) such as ibuprofen or naproxen for pain relief and inflammation reduction. 
 - Alternatives: Acetaminophen if NSAIDs are contraindicated or not tolerated. 
 - Medication safety considerations: Use NSAIDs with caution in patients with gastrointestinal, renal, or cardiovascular risk factors; avoid in patients with known allergies to NSAIDs. Acetaminophen should be used within recommended dosing limits to avoid hepatotoxicity. 
 - Return precautions: Advise the patient to seek medical attention if pain worsens, if there is new onset swelling, redness, warmth, numbness, tingling, weakness, or if systemic symptoms such as fever develop. 
 - Testing: Not indicated at this time given the typical clinical presentation; consider imaging or referral if symptoms persist beyond 6-12 weeks or if atypical features develop.

evidence.title: Tennis elbow - NHS
evidence.snippet: rvices without needing a referral from a GP. Get NHS help for back or joint problems Treatments for tennis elbow Tennis elbow usually gets better after resting your arm for a few weeks, but it can sometimes last longer. A GP may give you anti-inflammatory tablets. They may also refer you for physiotherapy if your symptoms have not improved after trying treat…

evidence.title: content.txt
evidence.snippet: cold pack (or a bag of frozen peas) wrapped in a towel on the affected area for up to 20 minutes every 2 to 3 hours - try doing simple exercises, such as bending and straightening your arm - try wearing a forearm strap or a wrist or elbow brace – you can buy these from pharmacies Important Do not smoke or go near naked flames if you're using ibuprofen gel be…

evidence.title: Elbow Injuries | Elbow Disorders | MedlinePlus
evidence.snippet: bone, cartilage, ligaments and fluid. Muscles and tendons help the elbow joint move. When any of these structures is hurt or diseased, you have elbow problems. Many things can make your elbow hurt. A common cause is tendinitis , an inflammation or injury to the tendons that attach muscle to bone. Tendinitis of the elbow is a sports injury , often from playin…

evidence.title: Tendinitis: MedlinePlus
evidence.snippet: onnect muscles to bones. They help your muscles move your bones. Tendinitis is the severe swelling of a tendon. Tendinitis usually happens after repeated injury to an area such as the wrist or ankle. It causes pain and soreness around a joint. Some common forms of tendinitis are named after the sports that increase their risk. They include tennis elbow, golf…

evidence.title: Broken arm or wrist - NHS
evidence.snippet: n arm or wrist It usually takes around 6 to 8 weeks to recover from a broken arm or wrist. It can take longer if your arm or wrist was severely damaged. You'll need to wear your plaster cast until the broken bone heals. The skin under the cast may be itchy for a few days but this should pass. The hospital will give you an advice sheet on exercises you should…
